_about: Adversarial evaluation set for the Aegis classifier. The 40 DESCRIPTIONS were written to be hard: hidden tier boundaries, distractors, systems that sound like one tier but belong in another. The DESCRIPTIONS are independent of the classifier. The GROUND TRUTH (expected_tier, expected_article) is deliberately left BLANK and must be filled in by a human reading the EU AI Act, NOT by the model that helped build the classifier. This separation is what makes the resulting accuracy number trustworthy.
_instructions: For each case: open the Act, decide the tier from the text, and record expected_tier plus the main expected_article (with page, e.g. 'Annex III, point 4(a), page 130'). The 'difficulty' tag tells you the KIND of trap to watch for, not the answer. If a case is genuinely hard to call even with the Act open, that is a finding: note it. Do NOT let the model fill expected_tier for you.

cases.expected_article: Annex III point 4, page 127 (ruled out: no decision about individuals)
cases.notes: Counts anonymous shapes to set staffing levels. Not recruitment/selection/evaluation of any person, so outside Annex III point 4. No Article 5 or 50 trigger.
cases.description: A retail company uses an AI tool that analyses anonymised, aggregated foot-traffic patterns from ceiling sensors to decide how many staff to roster each hour. It counts shapes, not identities, and stores no images.

cases.notes: AI systems intended to be used to make decisions affecting terms of work-related relationships, the promotion or termination of work-related contractual relationships, to allocate tasks based on individual behaviour or personal traits or characteristics or to monitor and evaluate the performance and behaviour of persons in such relationships.
cases.description: An HR platform scores employee engagement by analysing the tone and word choice in internal chat messages, flagging staff it predicts are likely to resign so managers can intervene.

cases.expected_article: Article 5(1)(f), page 51 (exception applies); Annex III point 1(c) ruled out via recital 18
cases.notes: Two independent reasons it stays low. (1) Article 5(1)(f) prohibits inferring emotions in the workplace EXCEPT for medical or safety reasons; nudging tired staff to rest is a safety use, so not prohibited. (2) Recital 18 expressly excludes physical states such as fatigue from the definition of 'emotion recognition', so this is not an Annex III point 1(c) high-risk emotion-recognition system at all, and the Article 50(3) emotion-recognition transparency duty does not bite either. CLOSE CALL: if 'stress' were read as an inferred emotion rather than a fatigue/physical state it could carry Article 50(3) transparency (limited-risk); minimal-risk is the defensible reading given the fatigue/safety framing.
cases.description: A wellness app for hospital staff uses the laptop camera to detect signs of fatigue and stress during shifts, and nudges the worker to take a break.

cases.notes: AI used to evaluate creditworthiness / establish credit score of natural persons. Squarely Annex III point 5(b). Not the fraud-detection exception.
cases.description: A bank uses an AI model to score loan applications from individuals and decide whether to approve consumer credit, using income, employment, and credit history.

cases.expected_article: Article 5(1)(a), page 51 (ruled out: no significant harm, not subliminal/deceptive)
cases.notes: Article 5(1)(a) prohibits subliminal or purposefully manipulative/deceptive techniques that materially distort behaviour AND cause or are reasonably likely to cause significant harm. Dynamic difficulty to sustain engagement is neither subliminal nor a source of significant harm, so the prohibition is not met. No Annex III or Article 50 trigger.
cases.description: A mobile game uses AI to dynamically adjust level difficulty to keep players engaged for longer, learning from how each player responds to near-failures.

cases.expected_article: Article 5(1)(h), pages 51-52 (exception (iii)); regulated as high-risk under Annex III point 1(a), page 127
cases.notes: Real-time remote biometric identification in publicly accessible spaces for law enforcement is prohibited under Article 5(1)(h) UNLESS a listed exception is met. Searching for a named suspect of a serious crime at police request targets exception (iii) (localisation/identification of a suspect for an Annex II offence punishable by at least four years). If the safeguards are met (prior judicial or independent administrative authorisation, fundamental-rights impact assessment, strict necessity and proportionality), the use is permitted and regulated as high-risk remote biometric identification under Annex III point 1(a) rather than prohibited. CONTESTED: tier flips to prohibited if the exception conditions/authorisation are not satisfied. Flag for review.
cases.description: A city pilots a system that uses live CCTV with facial recognition to find a specific named suspect in a public square, switched on only when police request it for a serious crime.

cases.notes: A chatbot intended to interact directly with natural persons (children) triggers the Article 50(1) transparency duty (inform users they are dealing with an AI), so limited-risk. The child-marketing angle was tested against the Article 5(1)(b) prohibition (exploiting age-based vulnerability) but does NOT meet the gate: 5(1)(b) requires the conduct to cause, or be reasonably likely to cause, SIGNIFICANT harm. The parent is the gatekeeper on spending, and 'keep playing / ask a parent for accessories' is not physical, serious psychological, or serious financial harm. This is the same significant-harm threshold that keeps E05 at minimal-risk; applying a stricter rule to the teddy than to the game would be inconsistent. AGGRESSIVE-READER NOTE: one could argue 5(1)(b) prohibition, but significant harm is not established on these facts.
cases.description: A toy company sells a talking plush bear with a built-in chatbot for children aged 4 to 8. It chats freely and encourages the child to keep playing and to ask a parent to buy accessories.

cases.notes: AI systems intended to be used to determine access or admission or to assign natural persons to educational and vocational training institutions at all levels.
cases.description: A university uses an AI system to score and rank applicants for admission to its degree programmes, producing the ranked list admissions staff use to make offers.

cases.expected_article: Annex III point 2, page 127 (ruled out: not a safety component in road-traffic management)
cases.notes: Annex III point 2 covers AI as a SAFETY COMPONENT in the management and operation of road traffic. Fleet route optimisation for fuel saving is not a safety component and does not manage road-traffic safety; no personal data about drivers or recipients. No Article 5 or 50 trigger.
cases.description: A logistics firm uses AI to optimise delivery van routes across a city to cut fuel use, based on traffic and parcel weight. No personal data about drivers or recipients is used.

cases.notes: Annex III point 4(a): recruitment or selection of natural persons, in particular to analyse and filter job applications and to evaluate candidates, including placing targeted job advertisements. Suggesting which candidates to contact is candidate filtering/evaluation for recruitment. A human making the final contact decision does not lift it out: the system materially influences the shortlist, so the Article 6(3) preparatory-task carve-out does not apply.
cases.description: A recruitment agency uses AI to write tailored job-advert copy and to suggest which of its existing candidates a recruiter might want to contact about a new role. A recruiter chooses who to actually contact.

cases.expected_article: Article 5(1)(g), pages 51-52 and Article 5(1)(c), page 51 (both ruled out)
cases.notes: Nearly hits two prohibitions and misses both. 5(1)(g) prohibits BIOMETRIC categorisation to infer sensitive traits; here the inference is from purchase history, not biometric data, so 5(1)(g) does not apply. 5(1)(c) social scoring requires detrimental/unfavourable treatment, in unrelated contexts or disproportionate; offering targeted discounts is a benefit, not detrimental treatment, so 5(1)(c) does not apply. No Annex III use (not credit, insurance pricing, employment, or essential public services). Inferring health data raises GDPR special-category-data issues, but that is GDPR, not the AI Act. Minimal-risk under the AI Act.
cases.description: A supermarket loyalty app uses AI to categorise shoppers by inferred characteristics, including likely health conditions inferred from purchases, to target discounts.

cases.notes: This is an AI-generated deep fake of real people. Article 50(4) requires deployers of deep-fake image/audio/video content to disclose that it is artificially generated; publishing 'without any label' is precisely a breach of that limited-risk obligation. The artistic/satirical carve-out only narrows HOW disclosure is made, it does not remove it. CONTESTED UPGRADE: if the synthetic video is intended to influence the outcome of an election or referendum or voting behaviour, it additionally becomes high-risk under Annex III point 8(b), page 129. As described ('political commentary'), the operative regime is the Article 50(4) transparency duty.
cases.description: A social media platform uses AI to generate realistic synthetic video of public figures saying things they never said, published without any label, for political commentary.

cases.expected_article: n/a (no Article 5, Annex III, or Article 50 trigger)
cases.notes: 'Tax categories' sounds official, but this only categorises a business's own expenses and flags misfiled items for a human bookkeeper. No decision about a natural person's rights, no Annex III use case, no prohibited practice, no transparency-triggering interaction. Minimal-risk.
cases.description: An accounting SaaS uses AI to auto-categorise business expenses into tax categories from uploaded receipts, flagging ones that look misfiled for the bookkeeper to check.

cases.notes: Annex III point 5(c): AI for risk assessment and pricing in relation to natural persons in the case of life and health insurance. Setting individual health-insurance premiums is exactly this. Customer consent to share activity-tracker data does not change the classification; the use case itself is high-risk.
cases.description: An insurer uses AI to set individual health-insurance premiums based on data including activity-tracker data the customer agreed to share.

cases.expected_article: Annex III point 1(c), page 127 (plus Article 50(3) transparency, page 82)
cases.notes: Inferring a customer's emotional state from voice is an emotion-recognition system (voice is biometric data; an emotional state is an inferred emotion under recital 18, not a mere physical state). It narrowly misses the Article 5(1)(f) prohibition because that prohibition is confined to the workplace and education institutions, and the subjects here are customers, not workers or students. It therefore lands at high-risk: Annex III point 1(c) lists emotion recognition, and recital 55 confirms emotion-recognition systems that are not prohibited are high-risk. The deployer additionally owes Article 50(3) transparency to the customers (an extra duty layered on the high-risk classification, per Article 50(6)).
cases.description: A call centre uses AI to detect the emotional state of customers (not staff) from their voice during support calls, to route angry callers to senior agents faster.

cases.notes: Textbook Article 50(1) case: an AI system interacting directly with natural persons. The duty is to inform users they are dealing with an AI 'unless this is obvious'; here it is obvious, so the obligation is satisfied, but the system still sits within the Article 50 transparency regime, i.e. limited-risk.
cases.description: A customer-support website has an AI chatbot that answers product questions. It is obvious to users they are talking to a bot.

cases.notes: Annex III point 3(d): AI for monitoring and detecting prohibited behaviour of students during tests in educational/vocational institutions. Remote-exam proctoring that flags 'suspicious' behaviour is exactly this. A human invigilator reviewing flags does not trigger the Article 6(3) carve-out: monitoring students during tests is the named high-risk use and materially feeds integrity decisions.
cases.description: A school uses AI proctoring software during remote exams that watches students through their webcam and flags 'suspicious' behaviour for a human invigilator to review.

cases.notes: Engagement-optimised film recommendation is not subliminal, manipulative, or deceptive within Article 5(1)(a), and causes no significant harm; 'increase watch time' is ordinary personalisation. Not an Annex III use case and no Article 50 interaction/content-generation trigger. Minimal-risk.
cases.description: A streaming service recommends films to users based on their viewing history, to increase watch time.

cases.notes: Social scoring. Article 5(1)(c) prohibits evaluation/classification of persons over time based on social behaviour where the score leads to detrimental or unfavourable treatment in social contexts UNRELATED to where the data was collected, or treatment that is unjustified/disproportionate. A general 'reliability score' built from fines, taxes and library fees and then used across multiple UNRELATED public services to set priority is the paradigm prohibited social score. NEAR-MISS: tempting to file under Annex III point 5(a) (essential public services eligibility), but the cross-context, generalised scoring crosses into the 5(1)(c) prohibition.
cases.description: A government agency uses AI to assign each citizen a 'reliability score' from their history of paying fines, taxes, and library fees, used across multiple unrelated public services to decide priority.

cases.notes: Annex III point 7(b): AI used by/for competent public authorities to assess a risk (including risk of irregular migration) posed by a person who intends to enter or has entered a Member State. Overstay risk is a risk of irregular migration; the rating feeds the visa decision. (Point 7(c), examination of visa applications, is also engaged.)
cases.description: A border agency uses AI to assess the risk that a visa applicant will overstay, producing a risk rating officials use when deciding the application.

cases.notes: Sounds like a 'safety component' or 'AI vision' high-risk case, but no natural persons are involved or imaged, and bottle-defect QC is not a safety component of an Annex I harmonised product requiring third-party conformity assessment (Article 6(1)) nor critical-infrastructure management (Annex III point 2). Minimal-risk.
cases.description: A manufacturer uses AI vision on the production line to spot defective bottles and divert them off the belt. No people are involved or imaged.

cases.notes: Two functions. Generating opening messages is an AI system interacting with users and producing synthetic text, triggering Article 50(1)/(2) transparency (limited-risk). The covert ordering of profiles to extract premium spending raises the Article 5(1)(a) manipulation prohibition, but on the same significant-harm logic applied to E05 and E07, ordinary commercial upselling (spending more on premium features) does not meet the significant-harm gate, so it is not prohibited. CONTESTED: an aggressive reader could argue 5(1)(a) dark-pattern manipulation; flag if the harness expects prohibited.
cases.description: A dating app uses AI to generate opening messages for users and also to subtly order profiles so that users it predicts will spend more on premium features see more matches.

cases.notes: Biometric categorisation by apparent age and gender for ad display, no identification of individuals. NOT prohibited: Article 5(1)(g) only bars inferring the listed sensitive categories (race, political opinions, trade-union membership, religious/philosophical beliefs, sex life, sexual orientation); age and gender are not on that list. NOT high-risk: Annex III point 1(b) high-risk biometric categorisation is, per recital 55, limited to attributes protected under Article 9(1) GDPR; age and gender (as distinct from 'sex life') are not Article 9(1) special categories. Recital 16 lists age/sex only as examples of what biometric categorisation can relate to, which engages the definition and the Article 50(3) transparency duty, not the high-risk threshold. So this is limited-risk under Article 50(3).
cases.description: A retailer installs AI cameras that categorise shoppers by apparent age and gender to display targeted advertising screens. It does not identify individuals.

cases.expected_article: Article 6(1) with Annex I (medical devices, Regulation (EU) 2017/745), page 53
cases.notes: High-risk under Article 6(1): an AI safety component of a medical device that automatically sets medication dosage. Medical devices are covered by Annex I Union harmonisation legislation requiring third-party conformity assessment, so both Article 6(1) conditions are met. (Not an Annex III route; this is the product-safety route.)
cases.description: A hospital uses AI software as a safety component in a device that decides medication dosage for patients automatically.

cases.notes: Generative AI drafting marketing copy. The Article 50(4) duty to disclose AI-generated text applies only to text published to inform the public on matters of public interest, and even then NOT where the content underwent human review/editorial control with a person holding editorial responsibility. Here a human edits and approves, and marketing copy is not a public-interest matter, so no deployer disclosure duty. (The upstream model provider still owes Article 50(2) marking, but the marketing deployer's use is minimal-risk.)
cases.description: A marketing team uses a generative AI tool to draft blog posts and social captions, which a human edits and approves before publishing.

cases.notes: Annex III point 4(b): AI to make decisions affecting terms of work-related relationships and to monitor/evaluate worker performance. Real-time productivity monitoring that automatically issues written warnings is both monitoring and an adverse work decision. 'No manager review before the warning is sent' means the Article 6(3) carve-out cannot apply: the system replaces, rather than supports, human assessment and materially affects the worker.
cases.description: An employer uses AI to monitor warehouse workers' productivity in real time and automatically issues written warnings when output drops below a threshold, with no manager review before the warning is sent.

cases.expected_article: Annex III point 8(b), page 129 (contested alternative: prohibited under Article 5(1)(a), page 51)
cases.notes: Annex III point 8(b) is a clean categorical hit: AI intended to influence the outcome of an election/referendum or the voting behaviour of natural persons, with recipients directly exposed (the admin/logistics carve-out does not apply). The prohibition route, Article 5(1)(a), requires manipulative techniques that cause or are reasonably likely to cause SIGNIFICANT harm. Treating 'harm to democratic participation' as clearing that gate would be more generous than the concrete physical/financial harm standard applied to E05, E07 and E22, so the consistent primary call is high-risk. GENUINELY CONTESTED (coin-flip): a reader who finds that 'exploiting each recipient's identified psychological vulnerabilities' to override autonomous voting clears the 5(1)(a) significant-harm bar would label this prohibited. Note 5(1)(b) does not fit cleanly, its vulnerabilities are age/disability/socio-economic, not generic 'psychological' ones. Flag for human review.
cases.description: A political campaign uses AI to micro-target persuasive messages, choosing wording designed to exploit each recipient's identified psychological vulnerabilities to change their vote.

cases.notes: Annex III point 2: AI as a safety component in the management and operation of critical infrastructure, including the supply of electricity. Managing electricity supply to a regional grid as a safety component is squarely this.
cases.description: An energy utility uses AI as a safety component managing the supply of electricity to a regional grid.

cases.notes: Grammar/spelling suggestions fall within the Article 50(2) carve-out for systems that 'perform an assistive function for standard editing or do not substantially alter the input data'. No content-generation transparency duty, no Annex III use, no prohibited practice. Minimal-risk.
cases.description: A spell-checker plugin uses AI to suggest grammar improvements as a user types an email.

cases.expected_article: Annex III point 5(b), page 127 (fraud-detection exception applies)
cases.notes: Looks like high-risk financial decisioning that affects access to money, but Annex III point 5(b) expressly EXCLUDES 'AI systems used for the purpose of detecting financial fraud' from the high-risk creditworthiness/credit-scoring category. No other Annex III point captures private-bank fraud detection (point 5(a) is essential PUBLIC services). So under the AI Act this is minimal-risk; sector financial-services rules and GDPR Art. 22 may impose separate duties.
cases.description: A fintech app uses AI to detect likely fraudulent transactions and freezes the account automatically pending review, affecting the customer's access to their own money.

cases.notes: Generating a realistic image of the consenting user is synthetic/deep-fake image content, triggering the Article 50(2) marking duty and the Article 50(4) deep-fake disclosure duty; the output is watermarked, so the obligation is satisfied. NOT prohibited: the user supplies their own photo, so there is no untargeted scraping of facial images (5(1)(e)) and no remote biometric identification of others. Limited-risk.
cases.description: An app lets users upload a photo and uses AI to generate a realistic image of that person in different outfits. Output images are watermarked as AI-generated.

cases.notes: Article 5(1)(e) prohibits creating or expanding facial-recognition databases through the untargeted scraping of facial images from the internet or CCTV footage. Scraping faces at scale from social media and CCTV to build a general search database is exactly this.
cases.description: A company scrapes faces from public social media and CCTV at scale to build a general facial-recognition search database it sells to clients.

cases.notes: Weather adjacency suggests critical infrastructure, but improving rainfall forecasts from satellite data is not a safety component in the management/operation of critical infrastructure and involves no natural persons. No Article 5 or 50 trigger. Minimal-risk.
cases.description: A weather service uses AI to improve local rainfall forecasts from satellite data.

cases.expected_article: Article 6(1) with Annex I (medical devices, Regulation (EU) 2017/745), page 53
cases.notes: A tool that analyses a selfie for signs of skin cancer and advises whether to see a doctor has a medical diagnostic intended purpose, which most likely makes it a medical device under the MDR regardless of the 'not a medical device' marketing label; classification follows intended purpose, not the vendor's disclaimer. If it is a medical device, it is high-risk via Article 6(1)/Annex I. CONTESTED: the whole call hinges on MDR status; if it genuinely falls outside the MDR (pure 'wellness' with no diagnostic claim) it could drop to limited/minimal. Flag for review.
cases.description: A health app uses AI to analyse a user's selfie for signs of skin cancer and tells them whether to see a doctor. It is marketed directly to consumers, not as a medical device.

cases.expected_article: Annex III point 1(c), page 127 (Article 5(1)(f) considered; Article 50(3) transparency)
cases.notes: Inferring disengagement from facial expressions is an emotion/intention-recognition system (recital 18: inferring an emotional state, not the mere detection of expressions). Emotion recognition not otherwise prohibited is high-risk under Annex III point 1(c), confirmed by recital 55; the deployer also owes Article 50(3) transparency. So the high-risk-vs-limited question is settled in favour of high-risk. The ONLY genuinely open question is whether it instead tips into PROHIBITED: Article 5(1)(f) bars emotion inference in 'workplace and education institutions'. A church youth group is most likely not a formal education institution, so it stays high-risk; if read as one, it would be prohibited. The subjects being minors heightens scrutiny. Flag for review.
cases.description: A church youth group uses an AI tool to detect, from facial expressions during sessions, which teenagers seem disengaged, so leaders can pay them more attention.

cases.expected_article: Annex III point 8(a), page 128 (also point 6(d), page 128)
cases.notes: A court using an AI reoffending-risk score in sentencing is high-risk under Annex III point 8(a) (AI used by/for a judicial authority to assist in applying the law to a concrete set of facts). The recidivism-risk function also maps to point 6(d) (assessing the risk of a person offending/re-offending). High-risk on either route.
cases.description: A court uses an AI system to help judges assess the risk that a defendant will reoffend, producing a score considered in sentencing.

cases.notes: Transcribing episodes and generating chapter titles is assistive content processing that does not substantially alter input semantics and does not produce deceptive synthetic content about persons. No Annex III use, no prohibited practice. Minimal-risk.
cases.description: A podcast app uses AI to transcribe episodes and generate chapter titles automatically.

cases.notes: Annex III point 3(b): AI to evaluate learning outcomes. Grading free-text essays and writing the grade to the official course record is exactly this. A nominal teacher-override that is 'rarely' used does not bring it within the Article 6(3) carve-out: the system materially influences the outcome and effectively replaces the human assessment.
cases.description: An online tutoring company uses AI to evaluate students' free-text essay answers and assign the grade that goes on their official course record, with teachers able to override but rarely doing so.

cases.notes: Estimating age from facial analysis is biometric categorisation, not biometric IDENTIFICATION (it does not establish who the person is), so it is outside Annex III point 1(a) and outside the Article 5(1)(g) prohibition (age is not a listed sensitive category). NOT high-risk under Annex III point 1(b): per recital 55 that category is limited to attributes protected under Article 9(1) GDPR, and age is not an Article 9(1) special category. So the deployer's duty is Article 50(3) transparency, i.e. limited-risk. The fully automated, no-staff-check access decision strengthens calls for scrutiny but does not change the tier under the AI Act's biometrics provisions.
cases.description: A bar uses AI face analysis at the door to estimate whether an entrant is over 18, refusing entry to those it judges underage, with no staff check.

cases.notes: Summarising the user's own typed notes is assistive processing of the user's own input; it does not generate deceptive synthetic content, does not affect any natural person's rights, and matches no Annex III use case. Minimal-risk.
cases.description: A note-taking app uses AI to summarise the user's own typed notes into bullet points on request.
